ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general okra food product entry; no specific entry for steamed okra with soy sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers okra as a vegetable commodity. No specific code for steamed preparation with soy sauce; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226734001 refers to okra as a food item; no specific code for steamed okra with soy sauce preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small plate of steamed lady fingers dressed with soya sauce as typically served in a Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Okra, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11279)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked okra (steamed/boiled, no salt). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect addition of approximately 5 ml soya sauce per 150 g serving (contributing ~350 mg sodium per 100 g of finished dish). All other macros reflect cooked okra values. Soya sauce contribution to energy, protein, carbohydrate, and fat is minor and within rounding tolerance for a condiment-dressed vegetable dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed okra with soy sauce. Okra is a non-starchy vegetable with very low digestible carbohydrate content and high soluble fibre (mucilage). GI of 20 is assigned by analogy with other low-GI non-starchy cooked vegetables (e.g., cooked green beans GI ~15, cooked carrots GI ~39) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Soy sauce addition does not materially alter GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (20 × 7.5 × 1.5 g available carb per 150 g serving / 100) ≈ 2.3.

hypertension.guidance: The soya sauce dressing contributes approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soya sauce or a low-sodium soy sauce variant, and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high soluble fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed lady fingers have a low GI of approximately 20 and provide 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, including viscous mucilaginous fibre that slows glucose absorption; this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile with beneficial fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed okra is virtually fat-free (0.2 g fat per 100 g, 0.03 g saturated fat) and cholesterol-free, and its soluble fibre may contribute to LDL cholesterol reduction; the primary cardiovascular concern is the sodium from soya sauce, which should be minimised for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Lady Fingers with Soya Sauce is a simple, clean Thai-style vegetable dish consisting of whole or halved okra pods (Abelmoschus esculentus) that are steamed until tender and then dressed with light soya sauce, sometimes garnished with fried shallots, chilli slices, or sesame oil. The dish is popular across Southeast Asia and appears on Thai restaurant menus in Singapore, where it is valued as a light, health-conscious side dish. The texture of steamed okra is soft and slightly mucilaginous, with a mild grassy flavour that is complemented by the umami saltiness of soya sauce. Nutritionally, steamed okra is low in calories (approximately 33 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat, and a good source of dietary fibre (3.2 g per 100 g), folate (46 mcg DFE), vitamin C (16.3 mg), and calcium (77 mg). The soluble mucilaginous fibre in okra has been associated with improved glycaemic control and modest LDL cholesterol reduction in clinical studies. The glycemic index is low (approximately 20), making this dish appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The main nutritional concern is the sodium contributed by soya sauce, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-containing (due to soy sauce), halal, and dairy-free. It is classified as a minimally processed vegetable dish.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for steamed lady fingers with soya sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked okra with sodium adjusted for soya sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for non-starchy cooked vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is well-supported and the food is nutritionally simple.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy cooked okra (NDB 11279) with sodium adjusted upward to reflect soya sauce dressing (~5 ml per 150 g serving). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as okra and soya sauce contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as okra and soya sauce contain no vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a vegan plant-based dish. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy cooked okra. 7. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Vegetables', which accurately reflects the primary ingredient (okra). 8. Cuisine is retained as Thai per the input record and the restaurant context (The Upper House - Funan, Thai menu). 9. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for this specific dish preparation is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 10. GL per serving = (20 × 11.25) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3 (available carbohydrate per 150 g serving ≈ 7.5 g × 1.5 = 11.25 g).
